_about: Keplerian orbital elements per JPL Solar System Dynamics 'Approximate Positions of the Planets' (Table 1, valid 1800–2050 AD). Each element is [value at J2000.0, rate per Julian century]. Angles in degrees. Earth uses Earth–Moon barycenter elements.
_rotation_about: rotation: [pole_ra_deg, pole_dec_deg, W0_deg, W_dot_deg_per_day] in J2000 inertial (ICRS), per IAU WGCCRE 2015 (Archinal et al. 2018). Pole's small T-dependent precession terms and periodic nutation corrections are dropped — sub-degree on 25-year horizons. Bodies without IAU values (Eris, Haumea, Makemake) leave the field absent and stay unrotated; their textures are fictional anyway.
_axes_about: axes_km: [a, c, b] triaxial semi-axes in km — a along mesh +X (longest equatorial), c along mesh +Y (polar), b along mesh +Z (intermediate equatorial). Runtime bakes a triaxial ellipsoid into the sphere geometry when present; otherwise uses radius_km as a uniform sphere. radius_km always stays the volumetric mean (used for orbit-arrival distance and search-distance display).

Mercury.notes: Smallest and innermost planet. Heavily cratered surface, no substantial atmosphere. Orbital period ~88 days, day length 59 Earth days due to 3:2 spin–orbit resonance.

Venus.notes: Earth's near twin in size and mass, but with a runaway-greenhouse CO₂ atmosphere ~92× Earth's pressure and ~462 °C surface temperature. Rotates retrograde — the only major planet that does.

Earth.notes: Only known planet to host life. Surface ~71% liquid water, plate tectonics, magnetic field generated by a liquid-iron outer core. One large moon (Luna).

Luna.notes: Earth's only natural satellite — fifth-largest moon in the Solar System. Tidally locked: same hemisphere always faces Earth. Formed ~4.5 Gyr ago, likely from debris of a Mars-sized impactor (the giant-impact hypothesis).

Mars.notes: Cold desert world with a thin CO₂ atmosphere (~0.6% Earth pressure). Polar ice caps, the largest volcano in the Solar System (Olympus Mons), and two tiny moons (Phobos and Deimos).

Jupiter.notes: Largest planet — more than twice the mass of all other Solar System planets combined. Gas giant with banded clouds, the Great Red Spot, and 95+ known moons including the four Galilean satellites Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto.

Saturn.notes: Second-largest planet, famed for its bright ring system of icy particles. 145+ known moons including Titan (denser than water, Mercury-sized, with hydrocarbon lakes) and the ocean-bearing Enceladus.

Uranus.notes: Ice giant tilted ~98° on its axis — likely the result of a giant impact. Pale cyan from atmospheric methane. 27 known moons, all named after Shakespeare and Pope characters.

Neptune.notes: Outermost planet. Deep-blue ice giant with the fastest sustained winds in the Solar System (>2000 km/h). Largest moon Triton orbits retrograde and is likely a captured Kuiper-belt object.

Ceres.notes: Largest object in the asteroid belt, the only dwarf planet in the inner Solar System. Diameter ~939 km, ~25% of the asteroid-belt mass. Surface ice and brine deposits suggest a subsurface salt-water reservoir; Dawn (2015–2018) imaged it in detail.

Pluto.notes: Largest known body in the Kuiper belt, reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006. Highly eccentric (e=0.25) and inclined (17°) orbit; spends 20 of every 248 years closer to the Sun than Neptune. Five known moons; Charon (~half Pluto's diameter) makes the system effectively a binary.

Eros.notes: Mars-crossing near-Earth asteroid, irregular peanut shape ~33.4 × 11.2 × 11.2 km. NEAR Shoemaker orbited and then soft-landed on it in 2001 — the first spacecraft to land on an asteroid.

Phobos.notes: Larger and inner of Mars's two moons. Tidally locked, orbits below the synchronous radius and so rises in the west and sets in the east — twice per Martian day. Likely a captured asteroid; will spiral into Mars or break up into a ring within ~50 Myr.

Deimos.notes: Smaller and outer of Mars's two moons, also tidally locked. Surface heavily cratered and dust-blanketed, smoother than Phobos. Orbits beyond the synchronous radius and rises in the east, setting in the west like ordinary moons.

Eris.notes: Most massive known dwarf planet (~27% heavier than Pluto). Highly eccentric (e=0.44), steeply inclined (44°) orbit reaching ~97 AU at aphelion. Its 2005 discovery prompted the IAU's 2006 dwarf-planet definition. One moon, Dysnomia.

Haumea.notes: Fast-rotating dwarf planet — a 3.9-hour spin stretches it into an elongated triaxial shape ~2200 × 1700 × 1000 km. Has a thin ring (the only confirmed ring around a TNO) and two moons, Hi'iaka and Namaka.

Makemake.notes: Third-largest known trans-Neptunian object and the second-brightest after Pluto. Methane and ethane ices coat its surface; expected atmosphere wasn't detected during a 2011 stellar occultation. One small moon, MK2.
